name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
قُلْ إِنِّى نُهِيتُ أَنْ أَعْبُدَ ٱلَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ قُل لَّآ أَتَّبِعُ أَهْوَآءَكُمْ ۙ قَدْ ضَلَلْتُ إِذًا وَمَآ أَنَا۠ مِنَ ٱلْمُهْتَدِينَ (٥٦)
```

```english
Say, ‘I have been forbidden to worship those whom you invoke besides Allah.’ Say, ‘I do not follow your desires, for then I will have gone astray, and I will not be among the [rightly] guided.’ (56)
```

```arabic
قُلْ إِنِّى عَلَىٰ بَيِّنَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّى وَكَذَّبْتُم بِهِۦ ۚ مَا عِندِى مَا تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ بِهِۦٓ ۚ إِنِ ٱلْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ ۖ يَقُصُّ ٱلْحَقَّ ۖ وَهُوَ خَيْرُ ٱلْفَـٰصِلِينَ (٥٧)
```

```english
Say, ‘Indeed I stand on a manifest proof from my Lord and you have denied it. What you seek to hasten is not up to me. Judgement belongs only to Allah; He expounds the truth and He is the best of judges.’ (57)
```

```arabic
قُل لَّوْ أَنَّ عِندِى مَا تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ بِهِۦ لَقُضِىَ ٱلْأَمْرُ بَيْنِى وَبَيْنَكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٥٨)
```

```english
Say, ‘If what you seek to hasten were with me, the matter would surely have been decided between you and me, and Allah knows best the wrongdoers.’ (58)
```

```arabic
۞ وَعِندَهُۥ مَفَاتِحُ ٱلْغَيْبِ لَا يَعْلَمُهَآ إِلَّا هُوَ ۚ وَيَعْلَمُ مَا فِى ٱلْبَرِّ وَٱلْبَحْرِ ۚ وَمَا تَسْقُطُ مِن وَرَقَةٍ إِلَّا يَعْلَمُهَا وَلَا حَبَّةٍ فِى ظُلُمَـٰتِ ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَا رَطْبٍ وَلَا يَابِسٍ إِلَّا فِى كِتَـٰبٍ مُّبِينٍ (٥٩)
```

```english
With Him are the treasures of the Unseen; no one knows them except Him. He knows whatever there is in land and sea. No leaf falls without His knowing it, nor is there a grain in the darkness of the earth, nor anything fresh or withered but it is in a manifest Book. (59)
```

```arabic
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى يَتَوَفَّىٰكُم بِٱلَّيْلِ وَيَعْلَمُ مَا جَرَحْتُم بِٱلنَّهَارِ ثُمَّ يَبْعَثُكُمْ فِيهِ لِيُقْضَىٰٓ أَجَلٌ مُّسَمًّى ۖ ثُمَّ إِلَيْهِ مَرْجِعُكُمْ ثُمَّ يُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (٦٠)
```

```english
It is He who takes your souls by night, and He knows what you do by day, then He reanimates you therein so that a specified term may be completed. Then to Him will be your return, whereat He will inform you concerning what you used to do. (60)
```

```arabic
وَهُوَ ٱلْقَاهِرُ فَوْقَ عِبَادِهِۦ ۖ وَيُرْسِلُ عَلَيْكُمْ حَفَظَةً حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا جَآءَ أَحَدَكُمُ ٱلْمَوْتُ تَوَفَّتْهُ رُسُلُنَا وَهُمْ لَا يُفَرِّطُونَ (٦١)
```

```english
He is the All-dominant over His servants, and He sends guards to [protect] you. When death approaches anyone of you, Our messengers take him away and they do not neglect [their duty]. (61)
```

```arabic
ثُمَّ رُدُّوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ مَوْلَىٰهُمُ ٱلْحَقِّ ۚ أَلَا لَهُ ٱلْحُكْمُ وَهُوَ أَسْرَعُ ٱلْحَـٰسِبِينَ (٦٢)
```

```english
Then they are returned to Allah, their real master. Look! All judgement belongs to Him, and He is the swiftest of reckoners. (62)
```

```arabic
قُلْ مَن يُنَجِّيكُم مِّن ظُلُمَـٰتِ ٱلْبَرِّ وَٱلْبَحْرِ تَدْعُونَهُۥ تَضَرُّعًا وَخُفْيَةً لَّئِنْ أَنجَىٰنَا مِنْ هَـٰذِهِۦ لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلشَّـٰكِرِينَ (٦٣)
```

```english
Say, ‘Who delivers you from the darkness of land and sea, [when] You invoke Him suppliantly and secretly: ‘‘If He delivers us from this, we will surely be among the grateful’’?’ (63)
```

```arabic
قُلِ ٱللَّهُ يُنَجِّيكُم مِّنْهَا وَمِن كُلِّ كَرْبٍ ثُمَّ أَنتُمْ تُشْرِكُونَ (٦٤)
```

```english
Say, ‘It is Allah who delivers you from them and from every agony, [but] then you ascribe partners [to Him].’ (64)
```

```arabic
قُلْ هُوَ ٱلْقَادِرُ عَلَىٰٓ أَن يَبْعَثَ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذَابًا مِّن فَوْقِكُمْ أَوْ مِن تَحْتِ أَرْجُلِكُمْ أَوْ يَلْبِسَكُمْ شِيَعًا وَيُذِيقَ بَعْضَكُم بَأْسَ بَعْضٍ ۗ ٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ نُصَرِّفُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَفْقَهُونَ (٦٥)
```

```english
Say, ‘He is able to send upon you a punishment from above you or from under your feet, or confound you as [hostile] factions, and make you taste one another’s violence.’ Look, how We paraphrase the signs variously so that they may understand! (65)
```

```arabic
وَكَذَّبَ بِهِۦ قَوْمُكَ وَهُوَ ٱلْحَقُّ ۚ قُل لَّسْتُ عَلَيْكُم بِوَكِيلٍ (٦٦)
```

```english
Your people have denied it, though it is the truth. Say, ‘It is not my business to watch over you.’ (66)
```

```arabic
لِّكُلِّ نَبَإٍ مُّسْتَقَرٌّ ۚ وَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ (٦٧)
```

```english
For every prophecy there is a [preordained] setting, and soon you will know. (67)
```

```arabic
وَإِذَا رَأَيْتَ ٱلَّذِينَ يَخُوضُونَ فِىٓ ءَايَـٰتِنَا فَأَعْرِضْ عَنْهُمْ حَتَّىٰ يَخُوضُوا۟ فِى حَدِيثٍ غَيْرِهِۦ ۚ وَإِمَّا يُنسِيَنَّكَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ فَلَا تَقْعُدْ بَعْدَ ٱلذِّكْرَىٰ مَعَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٦٨)
```

```english
When you see those who gossip impiously about Our signs, avoid them until they engage in some other discourse; but if Satan makes you forget, then, after remembering, do not sit with the wrongdoing lot. (68)
```

```arabic
وَمَا عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ يَتَّقُونَ مِنْ حِسَابِهِم مِّن شَىْءٍ وَلَـٰكِن ذِكْرَىٰ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَّقُونَ (٦٩)
```

```english
Those who are Godwary are in no way accountable for them, but this is merely for admonition’s sake, so that they may beware. (69)
```

```arabic
وَذَرِ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ دِينَهُمْ لَعِبًا وَلَهْوًا وَغَرَّتْهُمُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۚ وَذَكِّرْ بِهِۦٓ أَن تُبْسَلَ نَفْسٌۢ بِمَا كَسَبَتْ لَيْسَ لَهَا مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَلِىٌّ وَلَا شَفِيعٌ وَإِن تَعْدِلْ كُلَّ عَدْلٍ لَّا يُؤْخَذْ مِنْهَآ ۗ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُبْسِلُوا۟ بِمَا كَسَبُوا۟ ۖ لَهُمْ شَرَابٌ مِّنْ حَمِيمٍ وَعَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌۢ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَكْفُرُونَ (٧٠)
```

```english
Leave alone those who take their religion for play and diversion and whom the life of this world has deceived, and admonish with it, lest any soul should perish because of what it has earned: It shall not have any guardian besides Allah, nor any intercessor; and though it should offer every kind of ransom, it shall not be accepted from it. They are the ones who perish because of what they have earned; they shall have boiling water for drink and a painful punishment because of what they used to defy. (70)
```

```arabic
قُلْ أَنَدْعُوا۟ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا يَنفَعُنَا وَلَا يَضُرُّنَا وَنُرَدُّ عَلَىٰٓ أَعْقَابِنَا بَعْدَ إِذْ هَدَىٰنَا ٱللَّهُ كَٱلَّذِى ٱسْتَهْوَتْهُ ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينُ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ حَيْرَانَ لَهُۥٓ أَصْحَـٰبٌ يَدْعُونَهُۥٓ إِلَى ٱلْهُدَى ٱئْتِنَا ۗ قُلْ إِنَّ هُدَى ٱللَّهِ هُوَ ٱلْهُدَىٰ ۖ وَأُمِرْنَا لِنُسْلِمَ لِرَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٧١)
```

```english
Say, ‘Shall we invoke besides Allah that which can neither benefit us nor harm us, and turn back on our heels after Allah has guided us, like someone seduced by the devils and bewildered on the earth, who has companions that invite him to guidance, [saying,] ‘‘Come to us!’’?’ Say, ‘Indeed it is the guidance of Allah which is [true] guidance. And we have been commanded to submit to the Lord of all the worlds, (71)
```

```arabic
وَأَنْ أَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَٱتَّقُوهُ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ إِلَيْهِ تُحْشَرُونَ (٧٢)
```

```english
and that ‘‘Maintain the prayer and be wary of Him, and it is He toward whom you will be gathered.’’ ’ (72)
```

```arabic
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ بِٱلْحَقِّ ۖ وَيَوْمَ يَقُولُ كُن فَيَكُونُ ۚ قَوْلُهُ ٱلْحَقُّ ۚ وَلَهُ ٱلْمُلْكُ يَوْمَ يُنفَخُ فِى ٱلصُّورِ ۚ عَـٰلِمُ ٱلْغَيْبِ وَٱلشَّهَـٰدَةِ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْحَكِيمُ ٱلْخَبِيرُ (٧٣)
```

```english
It is He who created the heavens and the earth with reason, and the day He says [to something], ‘Be!’ it is. His word is the truth, and to Him belongs all sovereignty on the day when the Trumpet will be blown. Knower of the sensible and the Unseen, He is the All-wise, the All-aware. (73)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses are a continuation of the arguments against the polytheists (المشركين) regarding monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — and the related knowledge concerning prophethood (النبوة) and the Return (المعاد) — *the resurrection and the final judgment*. They possess a continuous and consistent context.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْ إِنِّي نُهِيتُ أَنْ أَعْبُدَ الَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ** (Say, ‘I have been forbidden to worship those whom you invoke besides Allah.’) until the end of the verse. He was commanded to inform them of the prohibition (النهي) issued to him against worshipping their partners (شركائهم); this is a prohibition against their worship expressed through a form of metonymy (الكناية).

Then He pointed to the basis of the prohibition against it by His saying: **قُلْ لا أَتَّبِعُ أَهْواءَكُمْ** (Say, ‘I do not follow your desires.’), which is that their worship is a following of whim (الهوى), and that has been forbidden. Then He pointed by His saying: **قَدْ ضَلَلْتُ إِذاً وَما أَنَا مِنَ الْمُهْتَدِينَ** (‘for then I would have gone astray, and I would not be among the guided.’) to the reason for refusing to follow whim, which is straying (الضلال) and exiting the community of the guided (المهتدين) — those who are characterized by the quality of accepting the guidance (هداية) of Allah, the Glorified, and are known by that. Thus, following whim contradicts the stability of the quality of guidance (الاهتداء) in the human soul and prevents the light of monotheism (التوحيد) from radiating upon the heart with a firm radiance that yields benefit.

From all this, a complete and reasoned explanation for the prohibition or the cessation of worshipping their idols is summarized: that in worshipping them there is a following of whim, and in following whim there is straying and an exit from the ranks of those guided by divine guidance (الهداية الإلهية).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْ إِنِّي عَلى بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّي وَكَذَّبْتُمْ بِهِ** (Say, ‘I stand on a manifest proof from my Lord and you have denied it.’) until the end of the verse. The manifest proof (البينة) is the clear evidence (الدلالة الواضحة) derived from *bayan* (البيان), which is clarity. The root meaning of this material is the isolation of one thing from another and its separation from it such that they do not connect or mix; from it come terms like *al-bayn* (the gap), *al-bawn* (the distance), *al-baynunah* (separation), and others. The manifest proof (البينة) is so named because truth is distinguished (يبين) by it from falsehood, becoming clear and easy to grasp without toil or burden.

What is intended by the referent of the pronoun in His saying: **وَكَذَّبْتُمْ بِهِ** (and you have denied it) is the Quran. The apparent context suggests that the denial (التكذيب) pertains to the manifest proof (البينة) upon which he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) stands, as is evident from the continuity of the meaning. This is supported by His subsequent saying: **ما عِنْدِي ما تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ بِهِ** (‘What you seek to hasten is not in my power.’) etc. For the sum of the speech, when this conclusion is joined to it, is: that which Allah has supported my message with of manifest proofs—namely the Quran—you deny; and that which you propose to me and seek to hasten of signs (الآيات) is not within my choice nor is its matter delegated to me. Thus, there is no common ground between us, for I have been given what you do not want, and you want what I have not been given.

From here it becomes clear that the genitive pronoun in His saying: **وَكَذَّبْتُمْ بِهِ** (and you have denied it) refers to the manifest proof (البينة) because what is intended by it is the Quran. Furthermore, His saying: **ما عِنْدِي ما تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ بِهِ** (What you seek to hasten is not with me) is intended to negate authority (التسلط) over what they seek to hasten through metonymy. For generally, regarding what a person is capable of—especially in the realm of giving and spending—that which he gives or spends is present with him or stored by him and under his authority, and then he spends from it what he spends. Thus, by His saying: **ما عِنْدِي** (is not with me), the negation of authority and power (القدرة) was intended by way of negating the necessitated (الملزوم) through the negation of the necessary consequence (اللازم).

His saying: **إِنِ الْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ** (Judgment belongs only to Allah) etc., is an explanation of the reason for the negation, which is why it was brought with negation and exception (الاستثناء) to signify restriction (الحصر). This indicates, by the occurrence of the negation upon the genus, that nothing of the nature of judgment (الحكم) belongs to any other than Him, the Exalted, at all, and that it belongs to Allah, the Glorified, alone.

## (A Discourse on the Meaning of Judgment and that it Belongs to Allah Alone)

The root of judgment (الحكم) indicates a type of precision (الإتقان) by which parts are harmonized and its flaws and gaps are closed, so that it does not fragment into parts nor dissipate into portions such that its effect weakens and its force breaks. To this returns the collective meaning among its various derivatives, such as perfecting (الإحكام), arbitration (التحكيم), wisdom (الحكمة), governance (الحكومة), and others.

Man has become aware of a type of realization of this meaning in master-servant duties and the rights circulating among people. For when masters and leaders command something, it is as if they are binding the obligation (التكليف) upon the subordinates and tethering them to it with a bond (عقداً) that accepts no dissolution and a tethering that leaves them no room for release. Similarly, the owner of a commodity or the possessor of a right in a matter has between him and his commodity or the matter a type of cohesion and connection that prevents another from intervening between him and his commodity by disposal, or between him and the object of his right, thereby restraining his hand. If someone disputes with the owner of a commodity regarding its ownership—such as claiming it for himself—or disputes with the possessor of a right, seeking to nullify his right, he has weakened this precision (الإحكام) and enfeebled this solidity. Then, if the judge (الحكم) or the magistrate to whom the case was raised affirms the ownership or the right for one of the disputants, he has created there a judgment (حكماً)—that is, a precision after laxity, and a strength of consolidation after weakness and frailty. His saying: "The ownership of the commodity belongs to so-and-so" or "The right in such-and-such belongs to so-and-so" is a judgment (حكم) by which the trouble of dispute and quarreling is removed, and no one other than the owner or the right-holder intervenes between the property or the right and its possessor. In short, the commander in his command and the judge in his judgment are as if they are creating a relation in the subject of the command and judgment, consolidating it thereby and removing weakness and laxity; this is what is called judgment (الحكم).

This is the path by which people became aware of the meaning of judgment in conventional, social matters (الأمور الوضعية الاعتبارية). Then they saw that its meaning is applicable to ontological, real matters (الأمور التكوينية الحقيقية) when attributed to Allah, the Glorified, in terms of His decree (قضائه) and measure (قدره). Thus, the fact that a seed, for example, grows in the soil, then extends into a stalk and branches, leaves and bears fruit; and the fact that a zygote transforms into a body possessing life and sensation—all of that is a judgment (حكم) from Allah, the Glorified, and a decree (قضاء). This is what we grasp of the meaning of judgment: the affirmation of a thing for a thing or the affirmation of a thing in the presence of a thing.

The theory of monotheism (التوحيد) upon which the Noble Quran builds the structure of its knowledge, since it affirms the reality of causality (التأثير) in existence (الوجود) for Allah, the Glorified, alone without any partner—even if the attribution differs according to the difference of things and does not proceed in a single manner, as you see that He, the Exalted, attributes creation (الخلق) to Himself and then attributes it in various instances to different things with different relations; and likewise knowledge (العلم), power (القدرة), life (الحياة), will (المشية), provision (الرزق), and beauty (الحسن), and so on—in short, since causality belongs to Him, the Exalted, then judgment (الحكم), which is a type of causality and origination (الجعل), belongs to Him, the Exalted, whether in ontological realities (الحقائق التكوينية) or in conventional, social laws (الشرائع الوضعية الاعتبارية). The speech of the Exalted has supported this meaning, such as His saying: **إِنِ الْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ** (Judgment belongs only to Allah) (6:57, 12:67), and His saying: **أَلا لَهُ الْحُكْمُ** (Look! Judgment belongs to Him) (6:62), and His saying: **لَهُ الْحَمْدُ فِي الْأُولى وَالْآخِرَةِ وَلَهُ الْحُكْمُ** (All praise belongs to Him in the world and the Hereafter, and judgment belongs to Him) (28:70), and His saying: **وَاللهُ يَحْكُمُ لا مُعَقِّبَ لِحُكْمِهِ** (Allah judges, and there is none to reverse His judgment) (13:41). If anyone other than Him, the Exalted, possessed judgment, he would be able to reverse His judgment and oppose His will. And His saying: **فَالْحُكْمُ لِلَّهِ الْعَلِيِّ الْكَبِيرِ** (So judgment belongs to Allah, the All-exalted, the All-great) (40:12), among other verses. These are specific or general verses indicating the exclusivity of ontological judgment (الحكم التكويني) to Him, the Exalted.

The exclusivity of legislative judgment (الحكم التشريعي) specifically to Him, the Exalted, is indicated by His saying: **إِنِ الْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ أَمَرَ أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوا إِلَّا إِيَّاهُ ذلِكَ الدِّينُ الْقَيِّمُ** (Judgment belongs only to Allah; He has commanded you to worship none except Him. That is the upright religion) (12:40). Thus, judgment belongs to Allah, the Glorified; no one else shares it with Him according to the apparent meaning of the aforementioned verses. However, He, the Exalted, sometimes attributes judgment—especially the legislative kind—in His speech to others, such as His saying: **يَحْكُمُ بِهِ ذَوا عَدْلٍ مِنْكُمْ** (as judged by two just men from among you) (5:95), and His saying to David (peace be upon him): **إِنَّا جَعَلْناكَ خَلِيفَةً فِي الْأَرْضِ فَاحْكُمْ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ بِالْحَقِّ** (O David! Indeed We have made you a vicegerent on the earth. So judge between the people with falsehood) (38:26), and His saying to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): **أَنِ احْكُمْ بَيْنَهُمْ بِما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ** (and that you should judge between them by what Allah has sent down) (5:49), and His saying: **فَاحْكُمْ بَيْنَهُمْ بِما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ** (so judge between them by what Allah has sent down) (5:48), and His saying: **يَحْكُمُ بِهَا النَّبِيُّونَ** (by which the prophets... would judge) (5:44), among other verses. Combining these with the first category yields the conclusion that true judgment (الحكم الحق) belongs to Allah, the Glorified, essentially (بالأصالة) and primarily (أولاً); no one else is independent (يستقل) in it. It exists for others by His permission (بإذنه) and secondarily (ثانياً). Therefore, He, the Exalted, considered Himself the Best of Judges (أحكم الحاكمين) and the Best of them, as that is a necessary consequence of essentiality, independence, and primacy. He said: **أَلَيْسَ اللهُ بِأَحْكَمِ الْحاكِمِينَ** (Is not Allah the fairest of all judges?) (95:8) and said: **وَهُوة خَيْرُ الْحاكِمِينَ** (and He is the best of judges) (7:87).

The verses containing the attribution of judgment to others by His permission and the like—as you see—are specific to conventional, social judgment (الحكم الوضعي الاعتباري). As for ontological judgment (الحكم التكويني), there is nothing in them—as far as I recall—that indicates its attribution to others, even though the meanings of the general attributes (الصفات) and acts (الأفعال) attributed to Him, the Exalted, do not preclude attribution to others in some manner by His permission and the like, such as knowledge, power, life, creation, provision, giving life, will, and others in many verses that need not be cited.

Perhaps this is out of respect for His side, the Exalted, because the attribute suggests a type of independence (استقلال) that there is no justification for attributing to these intermediary causes (الأسباب المتوسطة), just as ontological decree (القضاء) and command (الأمر) are likewise. Similar to this are the terms the Originator (البديع), the Maker (البارئ), the Creator (الفاطر), and other terms that follow the same course in suggesting meanings that imply a type of exclusivity. Use of them for other than His instance, the Exalted, was withheld out of respect for the sanctuary of Lordship (الربوبية).

Let us return to our interpretation of the verse. His saying, the Exalted: **إِنِ الْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ** (Judgment belongs only to Allah)—what is intended by judgment here is ontological decree (القضاء التكويني). The sentence is an explanation for the negation in His saying: **ما عِنْدِي ما تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ بِهِ** (What you seek to hasten is not with me). The meaning—as provided by the context—is that judgment belongs to Allah alone, and it is not for me to judge between myself and you; and that is what you seek to hasten by your proposal of a sign (الآية) to me.

Accordingly, His saying: **ما عِنْدِي ما تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ بِهِ** is used as a metonymy (الكناية), as if by their proposal to bring another sign besides the Quran, they were proposing to him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) that he judge between himself and them. Perhaps this is the secret in the repetition of the relative pronoun (الموصول) and the relative clause (الصلة) in the following verse where He, the Exalted, says: **قُلْ لَوْ أَنَّ عِنْدِي ما تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ بِهِ** (Say, ‘If what you seek to hasten were in my power...’). The apparent requirement of the context would have been to say: "If that were with me." This is because by His saying: **ما تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ بِهِ** (what you seek to hasten) in the first verse, the necessary consequence of the sign was intended—which is the judgment between him and them according to the divine sunnah (السنة الإلهية)—and in the second verse, the sign itself was intended. It is also possible that the matter of metonymy is the reverse of that, such that what is intended by "what you seek to hasten" is the judgment explicitly in the first verse, and the sign by metonymy in the second verse.

His saying: **يَقُصُّ الْحَقَّ وَهُوَ خَيْرُ الْفاصِلِينَ** (He relates the truth, and He is the best of those who decide). Asim, Nafi', and Ibn Kathir from the Seven [readers] read it with *qaf* and the undotted *sad* (يَقُصُّ) from *al-qass*, which is to cut something and separate it from another; from this is His saying, the Exalted: **وَقالَتْ لِأُخْتِهِ قُصِّيهِ** (And she said to his sister, ‘Follow him up.’) (28:11). The rest read it with *qaf* and the dotted *dad* (يَقْضِي) from *al-qada'* (judgment). The *ya* has been omitted from the script as in His saying, the Exalted: **فَما تُغْنِ النُّذُرُ** (but the warnings do not avail) (54:5). Each of the two readings has a basis, and they return to the same meaning, for relating the truth (قص الحق) and separating it from falsehood is a necessary consequence of judgment (القضاء) and ruling by the truth, although His saying: **وَهُوَ خَيْرُ الْفاصِلِينَ** (and He is the best of those who decide) is more suited to *al-qass* in the sense of separation (الفصل).

As for taking His saying **يَقُصُّ الْحَقَ** from *al-qass* in the sense of informing about something or in the sense of following a trace, as some commentators have suggested, it is not suitable for the context:

As for the first, it is because Allah, the Glorified, although He relates (قص) in His speech many stories of the prophets and their nations, the context here is devoid of that, so there is no reason to mention this attribute for Him and describe Him, the Exalted, by it.

As for the second, it is because the sum of its meaning would be that His sunnah, the Exalted, is to follow the truth and trace its path in the management of His kingdom and the organization of the affairs of His creation. While Allah, the Glorified, does not judge except by the truth and does not decree except by the truth, the etiquette of the Wise Quran precludes attributing following (الاتباع) and tracing (الاقتفاء) to Him, the Exalted. He, the Exalted, has said among what He said: **الْحَقُّ مِنْ رَبِّكَ** (The truth is from your Lord) (3:60) and did not say: "The truth is with your Lord," because of the trace of seeking support and the implication of weakness in the expression of "with-ness" (المعية).

## (A Discourse on the Reality of His Act and His Judgment, the Exalted)

His act and His judgment, the Exalted, are the truth itself, not merely corresponding to the truth or agreeing with it. To explain this: a thing is only "truth" (حقاً) if it is established in external reality (الخارج), occurring in the concrete world (الأعيان) without being invented by imagination or fashioned by the mind—like the human being who is one of the external existents, the earth upon which he lives, and the plants and animals by which he is nourished. A report (الخبر) is only "truth" if it corresponds to the established reality in itself, independent of our perception. A judgment (الحكم) and a decree (القضاء) are only "truth" if they agree with the sunnah (السنة) prevailing in the cosmos. Thus, if a commander orders something or a judge decrees something, the judgment of the former and the decree of the latter are only "absolute truth" if they agree with the absolute interest (المصلحة المطلقة) derived from the sunnah prevailing in the cosmos; and it is a "relative truth" if it agrees with the relative interest derived from the sunnah of the cosmos in relation to some of its parts without regard for the general universal system.

So, if a commander orders us to adhere to justice or avoid injustice, that is considered "truth" only because the system of the cosmos guides things toward their felicity (سعادتها) and their good. It has decreed for man that he live socially, and it has decreed for every society composed of parts that its parts harmonize, that they do not crowd one another, and that one side does not corrupt another, so that it may attain what has been apportioned for it of the felicity of existence (سعادة الوجود), and that this be distributed among its social parts. Thus, the absolute interest of this species is its felicity in life, and the command to justice and the prohibition of injustice correspond to it; so each of them is a true judgment (حكم حق). The command to injustice and the prohibition of justice do not correspond to it, so they are of falsehood (الباطل). Monotheism (التوحيد) is truth because it guides to the felicity of man in his real life, and polytheism (الشرك) is falsehood because it drags man toward a destructive wretchedness and eternal punishment.

Likewise, the judgment between two disputants is only truth if it agrees with the prescribed judgment (الحكم المشروع) in which the absolute human interest is observed, or the interest of a specific people or a specific nation. The real interest (المصلحة الحقيقية)—as you have known—is derived from the sunnah prevailing in the cosmos, whether absolutely or relatively.

It has thus become clear that the truth (الحق), whatever it may be, is only derived from the external cosmos, the system spread over it, and the sunnah prevailing within it. There is no doubt that the cosmos and existence (الوجود), with its system, sunnahs, and laws, is His act, the Glorified; from Him it begins, by Him it subsists, and to Him it ends. Thus, the truth, whatever it may be, and the interest, however it is conceived, follow His act and trace its path; they are established by reliance upon Him, not that He, the Exalted, follows the truth in His act and traces its path. He, the Exalted, is truth by His essence, and everything besides Him is truth through Him.

We, the community of humans, since we seek through our voluntary acts to complete the deficiencies of our existence and fulfill the needs of our lives, and since our acts sometimes correspond to our sought-after felicity and sometimes contradict it, we are compelled in that to observe the side of interest (المصلحة) which we acknowledge to be an interest—that is, it contains the rectification of our state and the felicity of our fortune. This led to the acknowledgment of prevailing laws and general judgments, and the consideration of social codes (شرائع) and sunnahs that must be observed and followed due to their fulfillment of human interest and their agreement with the sought-after felicity.

This led to the acknowledgment that interests (المصالح) and harms (المفاسد) have a real establishment (ثبوتاً واقعياً) and a domain of realization distinct from the two worlds: the mind (الذهن) and the external (الخارج)—isolated from the two abodes: knowledge (العلم) and the concrete (العين). They exert their effect in the external cosmos through agreement and contradiction. If our acts or our judgments correspond to the real interests established in the "thing itself" (نفس الأمر), the interest appears in them and they end in felicity. If they contradict them and correspond to the real, actual harms, they lead us to every injury and evil. This type of establishment is a real establishment not subject to disappearance or change. Thus, real interests and harms, as well as the attributes accompanying them that call for action or omission—such as beauty (الحسن) and ugliness (القبح)—and likewise the judgments emanating from them—such as the necessity (وجوب) of action or omission, for example—all of that has a real establishment that refuses annihilation and nullity, and is impossible to change or alter. They are governing (حاكمة) within us, prompting us toward such acts or diverting us, and the intellect (العقل) perceives these matters of the "thing itself" just as it perceives other cosmic matters.

Then, when they found that divine judgments and codes do not differ from human judgments and laws made in societies in terms of the meaning of judgment (الحكم), and likewise His acts, the Exalted, do not differ from our acts in terms of the meaning of act (الفعل), they judged that divine judgments and the acts attributed to Allah, the Glorified, are like our acts in their correspondence to real interests and their characterization by the quality of beauty (الحسن). Thus, real interests have an effect on His acts, the Exalted, and a governance over His judgments, especially since He, the Exalted, is Knower of the realities of things and Seer of the interests of His servants.

All of this is from an excess of opinion. You have known from what preceded that these are conventional, social judgments and sciences (علوم اعتبارية) and not real ones; we were compelled to consider and establish them by natural needs and the necessity of social life. There is no report of them in the external world outside the domain of society, and they have no value except that they are matters established in the domain of convention and consideration (الوضع والاعتبار) by which man distinguishes what benefits him of actions from what harms him, what rectifies his state from what corrupts it, and what makes him happy from what makes him wretched.

Sectarian bias (العصبية المذهبية) drove the two groups investigating religious knowledge in the early days of Islam to a strange opposition of excess and neglect in this regard. One group—the *Mufawwidah* (the Delegationists)—affirmed interests and harms of the "thing itself" and real beauty and ugliness that are established with an eternal, everlasting establishment, unchanging and unaltering, and which govern Allah, the Glorified, by way of necessity and prohibition, affecting His acts ontologically and legislatively through restriction and permission. Thus, they removed Him, the Exalted, from His authority and nullified the absolute nature of His kingdom.

The other group—the *Mujbirah* (the Necessitarians)—negated all of that and insisted that beauty in a thing is only the attachment of the command to it, and ugliness is the attachment of the prohibition to it; and that there is no purpose or goal in creation or legislation, and that man possesses nothing of his act nor has power over it before the act. Just as the first group went to the view that the act is created by man and that Allah, the Glorified, possesses nothing of man's act nor does His power attach to it.

Both views—as you see—are excess and neglect. It is neither this nor that; rather, the reality of the matter is that these and their likes are conventional, social matters (أمور اعتبارية وضعية) that have a real basis. That basis is that man—and similarly other social animals, each according to its measure—in his vital path, by which he intends nothing but the preservation of life and the attainment of felicity, is deficient and needy. He removes the aspects of his deficiency and need through his social actions emanating from consciousness and will. This compelled him to describe his actions and the matters to which his actions relate in the path of reaching the goal of his felicity and avoiding his wretchedness with the descriptions of external matters—such as beauty and ugliness, necessity and prohibition, permissibility, ownership, right, and others. He applies within them the laws of causes and effects, and consequently establishes general and specific laws, believing for them a type of establishment that he believes for real matters, so that the affair of his social life may be completed for him.

Thus, you see us believing that justice is beautiful (حسن) just as the rose is beautiful and lovely, and that injustice is ugly and hideous just as the stinking carcass is so; and that wealth belongs to us just as our limbs belong to us, and that such-and-all action is necessary (واجب) just as effects are necessary for their complete causes. On this basis, you see that these views differ among peoples if the goals of their societies differ. You see these people deeming beautiful what others deem ugly, and you find one group nullifying judgments that another considers, and you find a nation denying what another nation knows or being pleased by what another finds repulsive. Sometimes a sunnah is adopted, then taken, then abandoned in a single nation according to the stages of the social path and its contact with the requirements of life. This is in the goals that differ in societies. As for the general goals in which no two people differ—such as the basis of society, justice, injustice, and the like—their descriptions of beauty, ugliness, necessity, prohibition, and others do not differ at all, nor is there disagreement in them; this is regarding what pertains to us.

Allah, the Glorified, when He cast His religion into the mold of general social sunnahs, He considered in His explanation of real knowledge—cast in the mold of social sunnahs—what we consider in the path of our lives. He wanted us to think about what pertains to His knowledge and to receive the realities He conveys to us just as we think about and receive the sunnahs of life that we have. Thus, He considered Himself a worshipped Lord (رباً معبوداً) and considered us worshipped servants (عباداً مربوبين). He mentioned to us that He has a religion composed of original beliefs and practical laws that entail reward and punishment, and that in following it is the rectification of our state, the goodness of our end, and the felicity of our fortune, in the manner of the path we follow in our social views.

So there are original beliefs that we must believe in and adhere to, and there are practical duties and divine laws in worship, transactions, and politics that we must act upon and observe, just as the matter is in all human societies.

This is what permits us to investigate religious knowledge—whether creedal or practical—just as we investigate social knowledge—whether creedal or practical—and to rely in religious knowledge upon rational views and practical premises in the exact same way we rely upon them in social knowledge. Thus, Allah, the Glorified, does not choose for His servants duties and obligations except those in which there is the interest that rectifies their affair in their world and their Hereafter; He does not command except the beautiful and lovely, and He does not forbid except the ugly and hideous in which there is the corruption of religion or world; He does not do except what the intellect prefers, and He does not omit except what ought to be omitted.

However, He, the Exalted, reminded us along with that of two matters:

First: that the matter in itself is greater than that, and even greater. For all of that is knowledge derived from the materials of social views, and in reality, they do not exceed the stage of society, nor do they ascend to the world of heaven, as He said: **إِنَّا جَعَلْناهُ قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ ، وَإِنَّهُ فِي أُمِّ الْكِتابِ لَدَيْنا لَعَلِيٌّ حَكِيمٌ** (Indeed We have made it an Arabic Quran so that you may exercise your reason. And indeed it is with Us in the Mother Book, [and it is] surely high and wise) (43:3-4). And He said in a parable He struck: **أَنْزَلَ مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً فَسالَتْ أَوْدِيَةٌ بِقَدَرِها فَاحْتَمَلَ السَّيْلُ زَبَداً رابِياً وَمِمَّا يُوقِدُونَ عَلَيْهِ فِي النَّارِ ابْتِغاءَ حِلْيَةٍ أَوْ مَتاعٍ زَبَدٌ مِثْلُهُ كَذلِكَ يَضْرِبُ اللهُ الْحَقَّ وَالْباطِلَ** (He sends down water from the sky whereat the valleys are flooded to [the extent of] their capacity, and the flood carries a swelling scum. And from what they smelt in the fire for making ornaments or housewares [there is] a similar scum. That is how Allah compares the truth and the falsehood...) (13:17). And he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "We, the community of prophets, have been commanded to speak to people according to the capacity of their intellects," among other things mentioned in the Book and the Sunnah.

The meaning of this investigation is not the negation of beauty and interest, for example, from His acts, the Exalted, in the sense of affirming their opposites—such that it would entail the affirmation of ugliness and harm or the fall of His acts from rational consideration like the acts of children; Exalted is He above that. Just as the negation of sight in the sense of a physical organ from the intellect does not necessitate the affirmation of blindness for it or its fall from the rank of perception; rather, it is a sanctification from deficiency.

Second: that the aspects of beauty and the merits of interests, although His acts, the Exalted, and the codes of His judgments are reasoned by them, and the duties of servitude are explained by them—just as our rational judgments and actions are reasoned—there is a difference between the two doors. It is that in our case, they govern the will and affect choice. We, as rational beings, if we find an act possessing the quality of beauty joined to an interest not crowded by another, it prompts us to commit the action; and if we find a judgment of this description, we do not hesitate to legislate it, and we judge by it and apply it in our society, for example.

These facets and reasons—I mean the aspects of beauty and interest—are nothing but meanings we have taken from the sunnah of creation (التكوين) and external existence (الوجود الخارجي) which is separate from our minds and independent of us. We intended, in choosing beautiful actions possessing interest, not to stumble in our path and that our actions correspond to the sunnah of creation and fall into the path of reality. Thus, these aspects and interests are meanings abstracted from the external world of concrete things and are derivative of it; and our actions are derivative of these aspects, governed by them, and affected by them. The discourse regarding our established judgments is similar to the discourse regarding our actions.

As for His act, the Exalted, it is the external cosmos itself and the concrete existence from which we were abstracting the aspects of beauty and interest, and they were derivative of it since they were abstracted from it. How then can His act, the Exalted, be considered derivative of them, governed by them, and affected by them? Likewise, His legislative judgments, the Exalted, entail reality (تستتبع الواقع) rather than following reality (تتبع الواقع); so understand that.

It has thus become clear: that the aspects of beauty and interest and their likes, while they exist in His acts, the Exalted, and His judgments, and in our acts and judgments as rational beings, they differ in that regarding our actions and judgments they are governing and affecting—or if you wish, say they are motives and final causes (علل غائية)—and regarding His acts and judgments, the Exalted, they are necessary and inseparable—or if you wish, say: consistent benefits (فوائد مطردة). We, as rational beings, do what we do and judge what we judge because we intend by it the attainment of good and felicity and the possession of what we do not yet possess. He, the Exalted, does what He does and judges what he judges because He is Allah; and there follows from His act what follows from our act of beauty and interest. Our acts are questioned, reasoned by their goals and interests; His acts are not questioned, nor reasoned by a goal He does not possess, but rather are disclosed by their necessary consequences and necessary attributes. "He is not questioned about what He does, but they are questioned"; so understand that.

This is what His speech, exalted is His Name, guides to, such as His saying, the Exalted: **لا يُسْئَلُ عَمَّا يَفْعَلُ وَهُمْ يُسْئَلُونَ** (He is not questioned about what He does, but they are questioned) (21:23), and His saying: **لَهُ الْحَمْدُ فِي الْأُولى وَالْآخِرَةِ وَلَهُ الْحُكْمُ** (All praise belongs to Him in the world and the Hereafter, and judgment belongs to Him) (28:70), and His saying: **وَيَفْعَلُ اللهُ ما يَشاءُ** (and Allah does whatever He wishes) (14:27), and His saying: **وَاللهُ يَحْكُمُ لا مُعَقِّبَ لِحُكْمِهِ** (Allah judges, and there is none to reverse His judgment) (13:41).

If His act, the Exalted, were like our rational acts, His judgment would have a reverser unless it were supported by a beautifying interest; and it would not be for Him to do what He wishes, but rather what the accompanying interest indicates. And His saying: **قُلْ إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَأْمُرُ بِالْفَحْشاءِ** (Say, ‘Indeed Allah does not command indecency.’) (7:28), and His saying: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اسْتَجِيبُوا لِلَّهِ وَلِلرَّسُولِ إِذا دَعاكُمْ لِما يُحْيِيكُمْ** (O you who have faith! Answer Allah and the Apostle when he summons you to that which will give you life) (8:24), among other verses that reason the judgments by aspects of beauty and interest.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْ لَوْ أَنَّ عِنْدِي ما تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ بِهِ لَقُضِيَ الْأَمْرُ بَيْنِي وَبَيْنَكُمْ** (Say, ‘If what you seek to hasten were in my power, the matter would have been decided between you and me.’) until the end of the verse. That is: if I were capable of the sign you propose to me—and the case is such that if it were sent down to a messenger, it would not be inseparable from the decisive judgment between him and his nation—the matter would have been decided between me and you. Thereby, one of the two disputing, differing parties would be saved and the other punished and destroyed. No one would be punished thereby nor destroyed except you, because you are wrongdoers (ظالمون). Divine punishment only takes the wrongdoers for their wrongdoing, and He, the Glorified, is too sanctified for the matter to be confused for Him or for Him not to distinguish the wrongdoers from others, such that He would punish me instead of you.

In His saying, the Exalted: **وَاللهُ أَعْلَمُ بِالظَّالِمِينَ** (and Allah knows best the wrongdoers) there is a type of metonymy and reasoning; that is: you are the ones to be punished because you are wrongdoers, and divine punishment does not bypass the wrongdoers to others. In the sentence is an allusion to what preceded of His saying, the Exalted: **قُلْ أَرَأَيْتَكُمْ إِنْ أَتاكُمْ عَذابُ اللهِ بَغْتَةً أَوْ جَهْرَةً هَلْ يُهْلَكُ إِلَّا الْقَوْمُ الظَّالِمُونَ** (Say, ‘Tell me, if Allah’s punishment were to come on you suddenly or openly, would any be destroyed except the wrongdoing lot?’) (6:47).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَعِنْدَهُ مَفاتِحُ الْغَيْبِ لا يَعْلَمُها إِلَّا هُوَ** (With Him are the treasures of the Unseen; no one knows them except Him.) until the end of the verse. They mentioned regarding the connection of the verse to what preceded it that the previous verse, when it concluded with His saying: **وَاللهُ أَعْلَمُ بِالظَّالِمِينَ** (and Allah knows best the wrongdoers), Allah, the Glorified, increased in His explanation, mentioning that the treasuries of the Unseen (خزائن الغيب) or the keys to those treasuries are with Him, the Glorified; no one knows them except Him, and He knows every subtle and grand thing.

This explanation does not clarify the meaning of the restriction (الحصر) indicated by His saying: **لا يَعْلَمُها إِلَّا هُوَ** (no one knows them except Him). It is better to explain the connection by what the sum of the two previous verses contains—namely His saying: **قُلْ إِنِّي عَلى بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّي** until His saying **وَاللهُ أَعْلَمُ بِالظَّالِمِينَ**. For the whole indicates that what they were proposing of the sign and what it entails of the decisive judgment and decree between him and them is only with Allah; there is no path to it for anyone else. He is the Knower of that, the Judge of it, and He does not err in His decisive judgment and the punishment of the wrongdoers because He knows them best. Thus, He is Knower of the Unseen (الغيب) — *that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge* — no one shares it with Him, and He is Knower of everything that is grand or subtle; He does not stray nor forget. Then He increased that in explanation by His saying: **وَعِنْدَهُ مَفاتِحُ الْغَيْبِ** (With Him are the treasures of the Unseen), whereby He explained His exclusivity, the Exalted, to the knowledge of the Unseen and the encompassment of His knowledge of everything. Then He completed the explanation with the three verses that follow it.

By this, the verses proceed in the same manner as their counterparts in similar instances, such as His saying, the Exalted, in the story of Hud and his people: **قالُوا أَجِئْتَنا لِتَأْفِكَنا عَنْ آلِهَتِنا فَأْتِنا بِما تَعِدُنا إِنْ كُنْتَ مِنَ الصَّادِقِينَ. قالَ إِنَّمَا الْعِلْمُ عِنْدَ اللهِ وَأُبَلِّغُكُمْ ما أُرْسِلْتُ بِهِ** (They said, ‘Have you come to turn us away from our gods? Then bring us what you threaten us with, if you are truthful.’ He said, ‘The knowledge is only with Allah, and I communicate to you what I have been sent with...’) (46:22-23).

Then we say: **المفاتح** (*mafatih*) is the plural of *maftah* with the *fatha* on the *mim*, which is the treasury (الخزينة). It is sometimes considered possible that it is the plural of *miftah* with the *kasra* on the *mim*, which is the key (المفتاح). This is supported by the anomalous reading: **وعنده مفاتيح الغيب** (With Him are the keys of the Unseen). The result of the two meanings is one, for he who possesses the keys to the treasuries is knowledgeable of what is in them and capable of disposing of them as he wishes, usually like one who possesses the treasuries themselves. However, the rest of His speech, the Exalted, in what resembles this instance supports the first meaning, for He, the Exalted, repeated in His speech the mention of His treasuries and the treasuries of His mercy—and that is in seven places—and did not mention keys for them in any of His speech. He, the Exalted, said: **أَمْ عِنْدَهُمْ خَزائِنُ رَبِّكَ** (Or do they possess the treasuries of your Lord?) (52:37), and said: **لا أَقُولُ لَكُمْ عِنْدِي خَزائِنُ اللهِ** (I do not say to you that I possess the treasuries of Allah) (6:50), and said: **وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا عِنْدَنا خَزائِنُهُ** (There is not a thing but its treasuries are with Us) (15:21), and said: **وَلِلَّهِ خَزائِنُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ** (To Allah belong the treasuries of the heavens and the earth) (63:7), and said: **أَمْ عِنْدَهُمْ خَزائِنُ رَحْمَةِ رَبِّكَ** (Do they possess the treasuries of the mercy of your Lord?) (38:9). Thus, it is more likely that what is intended by the *mafatih* of the Unseen is its treasuries.

In any case, His saying: **وَعِنْدَهُ مَفاتِحُ الْغَيْبِ لا يَعْلَمُها إِلَّا هُوَ** is intended to explain the restriction of the knowledge of the Unseen to Him, the Exalted—either because the treasuries of the Unseen are known only to Allah, or because the keys of the Unseen are known to none other than Him, the Exalted, so there is no path for anyone else to those treasuries since he has no knowledge of their keys by which he might reach their opening and disposal.

The beginning of the verse, although it informs of the restriction of the knowledge of the Unseen to Him, the Exalted, its conclusion is not specific to the knowledge of the Unseen; rather, it informs of the encompassment of His knowledge, the Exalted, of everything, whether it be Unseen (غيباً) or manifest (شهادة). For every "moist and dry" is not specific to what is Unseen, and that is evident. Thus, the verse in its entirety explains the encompassment of His knowledge, the Exalted, of every Unseen and manifest thing, except that its beginning is specific to the explanation of His knowledge of the Unseen, and its conclusion informs of His knowledge of everything, more general than the Unseen and the manifest.

From another perspective, the beginning of the verse addresses the Unseen things that are located in the treasuries of the Unseen under the veils of hiddenness and the locks of ambiguity. Allah, the Glorified, has mentioned in His saying: **وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا عِنْدَنا خَزائِنُهُ وَما نُنَزِّلُهُ إِلَّا بِقَدَرٍ مَعْلُومٍ** (There is not a thing but its treasuries are with Us, and We do not send it down except in a known measure) (15:21) that the things in the treasuries of the Unseen with Him are matters that the witnessed limits (الحدود المشهودة) in things do not encompass, nor do the familiar measures (الأقدار المعهودة) restrict them. There is no doubt that they only became hidden Unseen things because of their quality of exiting the rule of limit and measure; for we do not encompass with knowledge except that which is limited and measured. As for the things in the treasuries of the Unseen, they are—before descending to the station of the manifest (الشهود) and dropping to the landing of limit and measure, and in short, before they exist with their existence measured for them—unlimited and unmeasured, while being established with a type of establishment with Him, the Exalted, according to what the verse speaks of.

Thus, the matters occurring in this witnessed cosmos, imprisoned in the prison of time, are—before their occurrence and origination—existent with Allah, established in His treasuries with a type of ambiguous, unmeasured establishment, even if we cannot encompass the modality of their establishment. So, among what is located in the *mafatih* of the Unseen and its treasuries are things before their origination and their settlement in their station measured for them among the stations of time. Perhaps there are other things stored and hidden that do not resemble the witnessed, familiar temporal matters we have. Let us call this type of Unseen—which does not emerge into the arena of the manifest—the Absolute Unseen (الغيب المطلق).

As for things after they are clothed in the garment of realization and existence and descend to their station with limit and measure, that which is within their limits and measures returns in reality to what is in the treasuries of the Unseen and returns to the Absolute Unseen. As for them, with what they possess of limit and measure, they are those whose nature it is that our witnessing (شهودنا) falls upon them and our knowledge attaches to them. When we know them, they become of the manifest (الشهادة), and when we are ignorant of them, they become Unseen (غيباً). It is appropriate that we call them, when they become unknown to us, the Relative Unseen (الغيب النسبي), because this description that occurs to them then is a relative description that differs by relations and additions. Just as what is in the house, for example, is of the manifest in relation to those within it, and of the nature of the Unseen in relation to those outside it. Likewise, the lights and colors perceived by the sense of sight are of the manifest in relation to sight, and of the Unseen in relation to the sense of hearing. The sounds attained by hearing are manifest in relation to it and Unseen in relation to sight. The percepts of both are of the manifest in relation to the human being who possesses them in his body, and of the Unseen in relation to other humans.

Those which He, the Exalted, enumerated in the verse by His saying: **وَيَعْلَمُ ما فِي الْبَرِّ وَالْبَحْرِ وَما تَسْقُطُ مِنْ وَرَقَةٍ إِلَّا يَعْلَمُها وَلا حَبَّةٍ فِي ظُلُماتِ الْأَرْضِ وَلا رَطْبٍ وَلا يابِسٍ** (and He knows whatever there is in the land and in the sea, and not a leaf falls but He knows it, nor is there a grain in the darkness of the earth, nor anything fresh or withered...) are of this Relative Unseen. For they are all limited, measured matters that do not refuse, according to their nature, that our knowledge attach to them or that they be witnessed by us; so they are of the Relative Unseen.

The verse has indicated that these matters are in a Manifest Book (كتاب مبين). So what is it of them that is in a Manifest Book? Is it these things from the perspective of their manifestation and hiddenness together, or is it from the perspective of their hiddenness only? In other words: is the Manifest Book this cosmos containing the concrete entities of these things, such that nothing of them is hidden from it even if some are hidden from others? Or is it a matter beyond this cosmos in which these things are written with a type of writing, stored in it with a type of storage, hidden from the witnessing of the witnesses among the people of the world, such that what is in the Book is of the Absolute Unseen?

In another phrasing: are the things occurring in the cosmos, enumerated in a general way in the verse, occurring by themselves in the Manifest Book just as lines themselves occur in the books we have? Or do they occur by their meanings in it, just as external objectives occur by their meanings with a type of occurrence in the pages and letters we write, and then the external corresponds to the correspondence of knowledge to the concrete?

However, His saying, the Exalted: **ما أَصابَ مِنْ مُصِيبَةٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلا فِي أَنْفُسِكُمْ إِلَّا فِي كِتابٍ مِنْ قَبْلِ أَنْ نَبْرَأَها** (No affliction visits the earth or your souls but it is in a Book before We bring it about) (57:22) indicates that the relation of this Book to external events is the relation of the book in which the program of action is written to the external action. Close to it is His saying, the Exalted: **وَما يَعْزُبُ عَنْ رَبِّكَ مِنْ مِثْقالِ ذَرَّةٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلا فِي السَّماءِ وَلا أَصْغَرَ مِنْ ذلِكَ وَلا أَكْبَرَ إِلَّا فِي كِتابٍ مُبِينٍ** (Not an atom’s weight escapes your Lord in the earth or in the sky, nor [is there] anything smaller than that nor bigger, but it is in a manifest Book) (10:61), and His saying: **لا يَعْزُبُ عَنْهُ مِثْقالُ ذَرَّةٍ فِي السَّماواتِ وَلا فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلا أَصْغَرُ مِنْ ذلِكَ وَلا أَكْبَرُ إِلَّا فِي كِتابٍ مُبِينٍ** (Not an atom’s weight escapes Him in the heavens or in the earth, nor [is there] anything smaller than that nor bigger, but it is in a manifest Book) (34:3), and His saying: **قالَ فَما بالُ الْقُرُونِ الْأُولى ، قالَ عِلْمُها عِنْدَ رَبِّي فِي كِتابٍ لا يَضِلُّ رَبِّي وَلا يَنْسى** (He said, ‘What about the former generations?’ He said, ‘Their knowledge is with my Lord in a Book. My Lord neither errs nor forgets.’) (20:51-52), among other verses.

Thus, the Manifest Book, whatever it may be, is something other than these external things with a type of otherness; it precedes them and then remains after their annihilation and expiration, like the written programs for actions that contain the specifications of the actions before their occurrence and then preserve the mentioned specifications after the occurrence.

Furthermore, these existents and events that are in our world are changing and transforming under the laws of general motion, while the verses indicate the impermissibility of change and corruption in what this Book contains, such as His saying, the Exalted: **يَمْحُوا اللهُ ما يَشاءُ وَيُثْبِتُ وَعِنْدَهُ أُمُّ الْكِتابِ** (Allah effaces or confirms whatever He wishes, and with Him is the Mother of the Book) (13:39), and His saying: **فِي لَوْحٍ مَحْفُوظٍ** (in a Preserved Tablet) (85:22), and His saying: **وَعِنْدَنا كِتابٌ حَفِيظٌ** (and with Us is a book that preserves [everything]) (50:4). The verses—as you see—indicate that this Book, while it contains all the specifications of events and the particularities of changing, transforming individuals, does not transform in itself, nor does any change or corruption penetrate it.

From here it appears that this Book is, in one way, other than the *mafatih* of the Unseen and the treasuries of things that are with Allah, the Glorified. For Allah, the Exalted, described these *mafatih* and treasuries as being unmeasured and unlimited, and that measure only attaches to things upon their descent from the treasuries of the Unseen to this world which is the level of the manifest (الشهادة). He described this Book as containing the subtle limits of things and the limits of events. Thus, the Manifest Book is, from this perspective, other than the treasuries of the Unseen that are with Allah, the Glorified; rather, it is something fashioned by Allah, the Glorified, that records other things and preserves them after their descent from the treasuries and before their reaching the station of realization, and after realization and expiration.

This is evidenced by the fact that Allah, the Glorified, only mentioned this Book in His speech to explain the encompassment of His knowledge of the concrete entities of things and the events occurring in the world, whether they are hidden from us or witnessed by us. As for the Absolute Unseen, to which there is no path for anyone other than Him, the Exalted, to be informed of it, He only described it as being in His treasuries and the *mafatih* that are with Him; no one knows them except Him. Indeed, some verses perhaps suggest or indicate the permissibility of others being informed of the Book but not the treasuries, such as His saying, the Exalted: **فِي كِتابٍ مَكْنُونٍ ، لا يَمَسُّهُ إِلَّا الْمُطَهَّرُونَ** (in a guarded Book, no one touches it except the pure) (56:78-79).

So there is nothing of what Allah, the Glorified, created but that it has in the treasuries of the Unseen an origin from which it draws support; and there is nothing of what Allah created but that the Manifest Book enumerates it before its existence, during it, and after it. However, the Book is a degree lower than the treasuries. From here it becomes clear to the clever, contemplative person that the Manifest Book—while being a pure book—is not of the nature of physical tablets and papers. For a physical page, however it is conceived and however it is measured, cannot possibly contain the writing of its own history for as long as it exists, let alone the history of others, let alone everything for all eternity.

It has been shown by what has passed of the investigation, first: that what is intended by the *mafatih* of the Unseen are the divine treasuries (الخزائن الإلهية) that contain things before they are emptied into the mold of measures (الأقدار); they contain the Unseen of everything to the extent indicated by His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا عِنْدَنا خَزائِنُهُ وَما نُنَزِّلُهُ إِلَّا بِقَدَرٍ مَعْلُومٍ** (15:21).

Second: that what is intended by the Manifest Book is a matter whose relation to all things is the relation of the book containing the program of action to the action itself. In it is a type of determination and measure for things, yet it exists before the things, with them, and after them. It is that which contains His knowledge, the Exalted, of things—a knowledge to which there is no path for straying or forgetfulness. Therefore, it is sometimes surmised that what is intended by it is the rank of the reality of things and their external realization which has no path for change; for a thing is not prevented from the occurrence of change upon it except after occurrence, and it is that which is said: "A thing does not change from what it occurred upon."

In short, this Book enumerates all that has occurred in the world of fashioning and origination (عالم الصنع والإيجاد) of what was, what will be, and what is, without anything escaping it. Yet, along with that, it only contains things from the perspective of their being measured and limited. Beyond that are tablets and books that accept change and transformation, and admit effacement and confirmation, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: **يَمْحُوا اللهُ ما يَشاءُ وَيُثْبِتُ وَعِنْدَهُ أُمُّ الْكِتابِ** (13:39). For effacement and confirmation—especially when contrasted with the Mother of the Book—only occur in the Book.

At that point, the connection of the verse—namely His saying: **وَعِنْدَهُ مَفاتِحُ الْغَيْبِ لا يَعْلَمُها إِلَّا هُوَ** until the end of the verse—to the verses before it becomes clear. For the sum of the two previous verses is that what you propose to me of signs deciding between me and you is not in my power, nor does the true judgment return to me; rather, it is with my Lord in His knowledge and power. If that were up to me, it would have been decided between me and you, and the punishment would have taken you—which takes none but the wrongdoers—because Allah knows that you are the wrongdoers, and He is the Knower who is ignorant of nothing. As for the fact that there is no path to grasping and having authority over what He wills and decrees of a deciding sign, it is because the *mafatih* of the Unseen are with Him; no one knows them except Him. As for the fact that He knows the wrongdoers best and does not mistake them for others, it is because He knows what is in the land and the sea, and He knows every subtle and grand thing, and all is in a Manifest Book.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَعِنْدَهُ مَفاتِحُ الْغَيْبِ لا يَعْلَمُها إِلَّا هُوَ** refers to the Absolute Unseen to which there is no path for anyone other than Him, the Exalted. His saying: **لا يَعْلَمُها** etc., is a circumstantial clause (حال), and it indicates that the *mafatih* of the Unseen are of the nature of knowledge, except that this knowledge is not of the same nature as the knowledge we are familiar with. For what comes to our minds from the meaning of knowledge is the form taken from things after their existence and their being measured by their measures. The *mafatih* of the Unseen—as has been shown—is knowledge of things while they are non-existent and not measured by their cosmic measures; that is, an infinite knowledge without being affected by the known.

His saying: **وَيَعْلَمُ ما فِي الْبَرِّ وَالْبَحْرِ** (and He knows whatever there is in the land and in the sea) is a generalization of His knowledge to what the knowledge of others can attach to—of that which some of it may be present to some and some of it may be hidden from some. He mentioned what is in the land first because it is more familiar to the addressees among people.

His saying: **وَما تَسْقُطُ مِنْ وَرَقَةٍ إِلَّا يَعْلَمُها** (and not a leaf falls but He knows it) was specifically mentioned because it is among what man finds difficult to attain knowledge of. For the extreme multiplicity found in the leaves of trees makes man unable to distinguish some from others, such that he might monitor each of them in the states that occur to it, and become aware of their decrease by the falling ones among them when they fall.

His saying: **وَلا حَبَّةٍ فِي ظُلُماتِ الْأَرْضِ وَلا رَطْبٍ وَلا يابِسٍ** — "nor a grain in the darkness of the earth, nor anything fresh nor any withered" (6:59), etc., are coordinated with His saying: **مِنْ وَرَقَةٍ** — "of a leaf" (6:59), according to the apparent context. The "darkness of the earth" (ظلمات الأرض) refers to its dark interior where grains settle; from them, some grow and others decay. Thus, the meaning is: no grain falls into the dark depths of the earth, nor does anything fresh or withered fall—whatever they may be—except that He knows it. Accordingly, His saying: **إِلَّا فِي كِتابٍ مُبِينٍ** — "but it is in a manifest Book" (6:59) is a substitute (بدل) for His saying: **إِلَّا يَعْلَمُها** — "but He knows it" (6:59), taking its place. Its implied meaning is: except that it is a reality recorded in a manifest Book.

Describing the Book as "manifest" (المبين), if it means "that which makes evident" (المظهر), is because it discloses to its reader everything according to its true reality, without any ambiguity of change or alteration, or the veil of obscurity affecting any of its attributes. If "manifest" (المبين) means "that which is evident" (الظاهر), it amounts to the same thing, because the Book is, in reality, the "written" (المكتوب), and the "written" is that which is being recounted. Since the reality is evident with no veil or obscurity over it, the Book is likewise.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَهُوَ الَّذِي يَتَوَفَّاكُمْ بِاللَّيْلِ وَيَعْلَمُ ما جَرَحْتُمْ بِالنَّهارِ** — **"It is He who takes your souls by night, and He knows what you do by day"** (6:60). **Taking the soul (التوفي)** means taking a thing in its entirety. Allah, the Glorified, uses it in His speech to mean the taking of the living spirit, as in the state of death, such as in His saying in the following verse: **حَتَّى إِذا جاءَ أَحَدَكُمُ الْمَوْتُ تَوَفَّتْهُ رُسُلُنا** — "until when death comes to one of you, Our messengers take him" (6:61).

He has considered putting one to sleep as "taking the soul" (توفيا), just as He considered causing death as "taking the soul," based on His saying: **اللهُ يَتَوَفَّى الْأَنْفُسَ حِينَ مَوْتِها وَالَّتِي لَمْ تَمُتْ فِي مَنامِها** — "Allah takes the souls at the time of their death, and those who have not died, in their sleep" (39:42). This is because both share the cessation of the soul's (النفس) control over the body. Similarly, "resurrection" (البعث) in the sense of awakening after sleep shares with "resurrection" in the sense of reviving after death the return of the soul to its control over the body after its cessation. Restricting the "taking of the soul" to the night and "resurrection" to the day follows the general norm that people sleep at night and wake up during the day.

In His saying, the Exalted, **يَتَوَفَّاكُمْ** — "He takes your souls," there is an indication that the spirit (الروح) is the complete reality of the human being, which one expresses as "I." It is not as we might imagine—that the spirit is merely one of two parts of the human being and not his totality, or that it is an accidental form (هيئة) or attribute (صفة) occurring to him. Even clearer in its indication is His saying, the Exalted: **وَقالُوا أَإِذا ضَلَلْنا فِي الْأَرْضِ أَإِنَّا لَفِي خَلْقٍ جَدِيدٍ بَلْ هُمْ بِلِقاءِ رَبِّهِمْ كافِرُونَ ، قُلْ يَتَوَفَّاكُمْ مَلَكُ الْمَوْتِ الَّذِي وُكِّلَ بِكُمْ ثُمَّ إِلى رَبِّكُمْ تُرْجَعُونَ** — "They say, ‘When we have been lost in the dust, shall we be in a new creation?’ Indeed, they disbelieve in the encounter with their Lord. Say, ‘The angel of death, who has been given charge of you, will take your souls, then you will be brought back to your Lord’" (32:10-11). The disbelievers' deeming it improbable is based on the idea that the reality of the human being is the body, which vanishes and decays through the dissolution of its composition upon death, thus becoming "lost in the earth." The answer is based on the fact that his reality is the spirit (الروح) or soul (النفس); since the angel of death takes it and seizes it, nothing of him is lost.

His saying: **وَيَعْلَمُ ما جَرَحْتُمْ بِالنَّهارِ** — "and He knows what you do by day." **Wounding (الجرح)** refers to an action performed by a limb (الجارحة), and what is meant here is "acquisition" (الكسب); that is, He knows what you have acquired during the day. It is more appropriate that the "and" (الواو) be circumstantial (حالية), making the clause a circumstantial qualifier for the subject of "takes your souls." His saying: **ثُمَّ يَبْعَثُكُمْ فِيهِ** — "then He raises you up in it" (6:60) then connects to His saying: **وَهُوَ الَّذِي يَتَوَفَّاكُمْ** — "It is He who takes your souls," without the intervention of an extraneous meaning. This is because the two verses are in the position of explaining the occurrence of divine governance (التدبير الإلهي) over the human being in his worldly life, at death, and after it, until he returns to his Lord. The fundamental core of the sentences listed to explain this meaning is His saying, the Exalted: **وَهُوَ الَّذِي يَتَوَفَّاكُمْ بِاللَّيْلِ وَيَعْلَمُ ما جَرَحْتُمْ بِالنَّهارِ ثُمَّ يَبْعَثُكُمْ فِيهِ** — "It is He who takes your souls by night, and He knows what you do by day, then He raises you up in it"—meaning in the day—**لِيُقْضى أَجَلٌ مُسَمًّى وَيُرْسِلُ عَلَيْكُمْ حَفَظَةً حَتَّى إِذا جاءَ أَحَدَكُمُ الْمَوْتُ تَوَفَّتْهُ رُسُلُنا وَهُمْ لا يُفَرِّطُونَ. ثُمَّ رُدُّوا إِلَى اللهِ مَوْلاهُمُ الْحَقِ** — "so that a specified term may be completed. He sends guardians over you until, when death comes to one of you, Our messengers take him, and they are not remiss. Then they are returned to Allah, their true Master" (6:60-62). This is the primary objective, and everything else is intended secondarily. The meaning is: He is the one who takes your souls by night while He knows what you have acquired in the day, then He raises you in the day, etc.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ثُمَّ يَبْعَثُكُمْ فِيهِ لِيُقْضى أَجَلٌ مُسَمًّى** — **"then He raises you up in it so that a specified term may be completed"** (6:60), etc. Awakening and alerting are called "resurrection" (بعثا) in parallel to calling sleep "taking the soul" (توفيا). The purpose of this resurrection is made the completion of the "specified term" (الأجل المسمى), which is the time known to Allah that the worldly life of a human being does not exceed, as He said: **فَإِذا جاءَ أَجَلُهُمْ لا يَسْتَأْخِرُونَ ساعَةً وَلا يَسْتَقْدِمُونَ** — "so when their time comes, they shall not defer it by a single hour nor shall they advance it" (7:34).

The completion of the specified term was made the ultimate goal because He, the Exalted, is the swiftest of reckoners. Were it not for a prior decree, He would have seized them for their evil deeds and the consequences of their sins, as He said: **وَما تَفَرَّقُوا إِلَّا مِنْ بَعْدِ ما جاءَهُمُ الْعِلْمُ بَغْياً بَيْنَهُمْ وَلَوْ لا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ مِنْ رَبِّكَ إِلى أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى لَقُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ** — "They did not divide except after knowledge had come to them, out of envy among themselves; and were it not for a prior word from your Lord [deferring them] until a specified term, the matter would have been decided between them" (42:14). The prior decree is that which is contained in His saying, the Exalted, in the story of the descent of Adam (peace be upon him): **وَلَكُمْ فِي الْأَنْفُسِ مُسْتَقَرٌّ وَمَتاعٌ إِلى حِينٍ** — "There will be for you on the earth an abode and enjoyment for a while" (7:24).

The meaning, then, is that Allah takes your souls by night while He knows the evil deeds and other things you have acquired in the day, but He does not retain your spirits to prolong death upon them; rather, He raises you in the day after the taking of the soul so that your specified terms may be completed. Then to Him is your return through the descent of death and the gathering (الحشر), and He will inform you of what you used to do.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَهُوَ الْقاهِرُ فَوْقَ عِبادِهِ** — **"He is the All-Dominant over His servants"** (6:61). The discussion regarding this has already preceded in the commentary (بيان) on verse 17 of this Surah.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَيُرْسِلُ عَلَيْكُمْ حَفَظَةً** — **"and He sends guardians over you"** (6:61), etc. The absolute mention of sending guardians without restriction—neither in the sending nor in the guardians—and then making it bounded by the arrival of death, is not without indication that these sent guardians have the function of protecting the human being from every calamity that heads toward him, every misfortune that seeks him out, and every blight that aims for him. For the realm (النشأة) we are in is a realm of interaction (التفاعل) and conflict (التزاحم); there is nothing in it except that it is afflicted by the conflict of something else from all directions, because every part of this natural world is in pursuit of perfection (الاستكمال) and increasing its share of existence (الوجود). Nothing increases in one thing except that it decreases proportionately in another; thus, things are always in a state of contention and overcoming. Among its parts is the human being, whose existential composition is the most subtle and precise of compositions within it, as far as we know. Therefore, his rivals in existence are more numerous and his enemies in life are more dangerous. So Allah sent to him guardians from among the angels who protect him from the occurrences of accidents and the assaults of calamities and misfortunes. They continue to protect him from destruction until, when his term comes, they leave him to the calamity, and it destroys him, as mentioned in the narrations.

As for what He mentioned in His saying: **إِنَّ عَلَيْكُمْ لَحافِظِينَ. كِراماً كاتِبِينَ. يَعْلَمُونَ ما تَفْعَلُونَ** — "Indeed, there are over you guardians, noble writers, who know what you do" (82:10-12), He intends by it the guardians over deeds. However, some have taken those verses as an explanation for this verse. While the verse does not entirely refuse this meaning, His saying: **حَتَّى إِذا جاءَ أَحَدَكُمُ الْمَوْتُ** — "until, when death comes to one of you" (6:61) until the end of the verse, as previously mentioned, supports the first meaning.

His saying: **تَوَفَّتْهُ رُسُلُنا وَهُمْ لا يُفَرِّطُونَ** — "Our messengers take him, and they are not remiss" (6:61). It appears that what is meant by "being remiss" (التفريط) is negligence or leniency in executing the divine command of taking the soul. For Allah, the Glorified, described His angels as doing what they are commanded, and mentioned that every nation is pledged to its term; when their term comes, they shall not defer it by an hour nor advance it. Thus, the angels charged with the matter of taking the soul do not fall short of the necessary, defined limit disclosed to them—that so-and-so dies at such-and-such an hour under such-and-such conditions. They are not lenient in taking the soul of the one they were commanded to take, not even by an atom's weight; thus, they are not remiss.

Are these messengers the same messengers mentioned first, such that the guardians are those entrusted with taking the soul? The verse is silent on that, except for a weak suggestion of unity. However, these messengers commanded to take the soul, whoever they may be, are among the assistants of the Angel of Death, due to His saying, the Exalted: **قُل يَتَوَفَّاكُمْ مَلَكُ الْمَوْتِ الَّذِي وُكِّلَ بِكُمْ** — "Say, ‘The angel of death, who has been given charge of you, will take your souls’" (32:11).

The attribution of taking the soul to these messengers, then to the Angel of Death in the verse just cited, then to Allah, the Glorified, in His saying: **اللهُ يَتَوَفَّى الْأَنْفُسَ** — "Allah takes the souls" (39:42), is a matter of variation in the levels of attribution (النسب). For every matter ultimately returns to Allah, the Glorified, and He is the absolute Owner and Controller. The Angel of Death employs assistants to accomplish what he does in seizing spirits, and they are the causes (الأسباب), means (الوسائل), and instruments (الأدوات) of the act—like the line drawn by the pen, behind which is the hand, and behind them both is the human writer.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ثُمَّ رُدُّوا إِلَى اللهِ مَوْلاهُمُ الْحَقِ** — **"Then they are returned to Allah, their true Master"** (6:62). This is an allusion to their return to Allah, the Glorified, through the resurrection after death. Describing Him, the Exalted, as their "true Master" (مولاهم الحق) is to indicate the cause of all His aforementioned actions—putting to sleep, awakening, governing, causing death, and resurrecting. In this is an analysis of the meaning of "Master" (المولى) and then an affirmation of the right of mastership (المولوية) for Him, the Exalted. The Master is the one who owns the essence (الرقبة), and thus it is His right to dispose of it however He wills. Since He, the Exalted, possesses the reality of ownership (حقيقة الملك), and He is the one who acts through creation (الإيجاد), governance (التدبير), and returning (الإرجاع), He is the true Master for whom the meaning of mastership is established with a permanence that never vanishes in any way.

"The Truth" (الحق) is among the Beautiful Names of Allah because of His establishment, the Exalted, in His essence and attributes with a permanence that accepts no cessation and is impossible to change or transfer. The pronoun in **رُدُّوا** — "they are returned" refers to the individuals alluded to in the preceding speech: **حَتَّى إِذا جاءَ أَحَدَكُمُ الْمَوْتُ** — "until, when death comes to one of you" (6:61). For the decree of death encompasses every individual, and through it, their individuals are gathered into the group itself. From here it becomes clear that His saying: **ثُمَّ رُدُّوا** — "Then they are returned" is not a case of transition (الالتفات) from the previous second-person address to the third-person.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَلا لَهُ الْحُكْمُ** — **"Look! All judgment belongs to Him"** (6:62), etc. When He, the Exalted, explained His exclusive possession of the keys of the Unseen, His knowledge of the manifest Book in which everything is recorded, and His governance of the affairs of His creation from the moment they existed until they return to Him, it became clear that judgment belongs to Him and not to anyone else. This is what He mentioned previously in His saying: **إِنِ الْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ** — "Judgment belongs only to Allah" (6:57). He announced the result of His explanation, saying: **أَلا لَهُ الْحُكْمُ** — "Look! All judgment belongs to Him," to alert them to what they had neglected.

Similarly, His saying: **وَهُوَ أَسْرَعُ الْحاسِبِينَ** — "and He is the swiftest of reckoners" (6:62) is another result of the preceding explanation. For it has become clear through it that He, the Exalted, does not delay the reckoning of people's deeds beyond the time suitable for it; rather, what is delayed is only delayed to reach the term that was appointed for it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْ مَنْ يُنَجِّيكُمْ مِنْ ظُلُماتِ الْبَرِّ وَالْبَحْرِ تَدْعُونَهُ** — **"Say, ‘Who delivers you from the darkness of land and sea, [when] you call Him’"** (6:63) until the end of the verse. It seems that what is meant by deliverance from the "darkness of land and sea" is salvation from the hardships that afflict a human being during travels, whether traversing the land or sailing the sea—such as severe cold, rain, snow, highwaymen, storms, and the like.
